question: A Swiggy restaurant dataset has 40% missing values in 'avg_delivery_rating'. What is the safest first step?
options: Drop all rows with missing ratings — cleaner data beats imputed data
Fill with the column mean — always the safest default
Understand WHY data is missing first — then decide: median if skewed, model-based if a pattern exists, drop only if missingness itself is informative
Replace missing values with 0 — neutral fill
explanation: Missing data has a reason. MCAR (missing completely at random) → impute safely with mean/median. MAR (new restaurants have no ratings yet) → model-based imputation using other columns. MNAR (missing IS the signal — low-rated places remove their rating) → encode 'was_missing' as a binary feature. Never blindly fill or drop without understanding the pattern.

question: Features: age (22–30), study_hours (4–12), salary_LPA (6–40). You train a k-NN classifier without scaling. What happens?
options: Nothing — k-NN is scale-invariant like tree models
salary_LPA dominates all distance calculations, making age and study_hours irrelevant to the model
The model trains slower but final accuracy is unaffected
Scaling only matters for neural networks, not classical ML
explanation: k-NN uses Euclidean distance. A salary difference of ₹1 LPA = 100,000 units vs age difference of 1 year = 1 unit. Salary completely drowns the other features — the model effectively only uses salary. After StandardScaler, all features contribute equally. Exception: tree models (Decision Tree, Random Forest) split on thresholds, not distances — they don't need scaling.

question: A city column has 8 unique values: Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore... You're building logistic regression. Which encoding is correct?
options: Label Encoding (Delhi=0, Mumbai=1, Bangalore=2...) — simpler and works for all models
One-Hot Encoding — creates a binary column per city, avoids implying a false ordering between cities
Target Encoding — replace each city with its mean target value
Drop the column — categorical strings cannot enter logistic regression
explanation: Label Encoding on nominal (unordered) categories tells logistic regression that Mumbai (1) is 'half of' Bangalore (2) — a false mathematical relationship. One-Hot Encoding avoids this: each city gets its own binary column. Exception: tree models handle label encoding fine since they split on specific values, not magnitudes. Target Encoding works but risks leaking label info — use with cross-fold encoding.

question: Your Razorpay fraud model: training accuracy 99.4%, test accuracy on new data 59%. Feature set includes 'fraud_case_opened' — a flag set after a fraud is suspected. What is happening?
options: Overfitting — add L2 regularisation to reduce variance
Underfitting — the model needs more features to learn
Data leakage — 'fraud_case_opened' is set after fraud is confirmed, so it encodes the answer during training but is always 0 at prediction time
Class imbalance — use SMOTE to fix the label distribution
explanation: Leakage: using information unavailable at prediction time. 'fraud_case_opened' is only set after fraud is detected — so in training it perfectly predicts fraud (hence 99.4%), but in production it's always 0 for new transactions (hence 59%). Rule: every training feature must exist at the exact moment the model makes a prediction. Time-based features computed on the full dataset before splitting also cause leakage.

question: When should you prefer StandardScaler over MinMaxScaler?
options: Always use MinMaxScaler — the 0 to 1 range is universally better
StandardScaler when features have outliers or you're using SVM, PCA, or linear models. MinMaxScaler when you need bounded output like neural networks with sigmoid/tanh or image pixel data.
They produce identical results — pick either one
Neither is needed — only neural networks require feature scaling
explanation: StandardScaler (z-score: mean=0, std=1): robust to outliers — a ₹50L salary outlier won't compress all other values into a tiny range. Preferred for SVM, PCA, logistic regression. MinMaxScaler (0–1 range): sensitive to outliers but gives bounded output — preferred for sigmoid/tanh activations in neural networks, and image pixels (already 0–255). Neither is needed for tree-based models (Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost) which split on thresholds.
